reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
Real-world applications frequently provide challenges that are not seen in common computer vision datasets like Imagenet, where images are blurry, dark, corrupted, objects are highly occluded, test objects may be out of distribution due to natural distribution shifts, etc.  This phenomenon was investigated in 2018 by Beery et al, who similarly found categorization is easier with localization in challenging real-world scenes (ie classifying cropped boxes). I would recommend taking a look at that paper (citation below) and including it in the related work.

Beery, S., Van Horn, G., & Perona, P. (2018). Recognition in terra incognita. In Proceedings of the European Conference on Computer Vision (ECCV) (pp. 456-473).


Pros:

The authors provide many experiments digging into various aspects of what makes real-world object detection challenging. This is a useful reference point for future work.

Cons:
This paper re-analyzes generalization in the context of an existing dataset. There is not anything particularly novel about the analysis, and similar results have been shown on other real-world datasets.
The authors describe the image distortions they consider to be “natural”, but are applying them synthetically. It is not clear to me that applying a synthetic distortion of a type that can be seen in the real world is necessarily reflective of those realistic distortions in the wild.  It would be better to explicitly collect examples of these types of distortions in real data and compare against that.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Expanded Review

Real-world applications frequently provide challenges that are not seen in common computer vision datasets like ImageNet, where images are blurry, dark, corrupted, objects are highly occluded, test objects may be out of distribution due to natural distribution shifts, and viewing conditions vary dramatically from controlled laboratory settings. This phenomenon was investigated in 2018 by Beery et al, who similarly found categorization is easier with localization in challenging real-world scenes (i.e., classifying cropped boxes). The authors' focus on ObjectNet aligns well with this prior work, and I would recommend taking a closer look at that paper (citation below) and including it more prominently in the related work section, as it provides important precedent for the core findings presented here.

Beery, S., Van Horn, G., & Perona, P. (2018). Recognition in terra incognita. In Proceedings of the European Conference on Computer Vision (ECCV) (pp. 456-473).

## Strengths

The authors provide a comprehensive set of experiments digging into various aspects of what makes real-world object recognition challenging. The investigation spans multiple dimensions of the problem—from natural distribution shifts through object isolation, to synthetic perturbations including geometric transformations, common image corruptions, and adversarial attacks. This breadth of analysis is commendable and creates a useful reference point for future work in understanding model generalization. The manual annotation of 18,574 bounding boxes across 113 categories represents a significant effort that will likely be valuable for the community.

The paper makes a clear methodological contribution by highlighting an important confound in the original ObjectNet analysis: the distinction between object recognition and scene understanding. The authors correctly identify that applying pre-trained models to cluttered multi-object scenes differs fundamentally from the single-object recognition task that ImageNet models were designed for. This observation is important and helps clarify the interpretation of the dramatic performance drops reported in the original ObjectNet paper. The finding that 20-30% of the performance loss can be recovered by using isolated objects (bounding boxes) provides a more nuanced understanding of where the generalization gap truly lies.

Additionally, the investigation of segmentation masks versus bounding boxes (Section 3.3) is interesting, showing consistent improvements in robustness across different types of perturbations. The experiments on MNIST and Fashion-MNIST with background subtraction (Section 3.3 and Appendix G) provide controlled evidence for the hypothesis that irrelevant background information contributes to model vulnerability. The qualitative analysis in Section 3.4, including the human difficulty assessment and the observation that easy/hard samples for models align with easy/hard samples for humans, provides valuable anecdotal evidence about the nature of ObjectNet's challenges.

## Weaknesses

### Limited Novelty in Core Findings

This paper re-analyzes generalization in the context of an existing dataset (ObjectNet) rather than proposing new methods, datasets, or theoretical insights. While the re-analysis is thorough, the core finding—that applying models to cropped objects rather than full scenes improves performance—is not particularly novel. As noted, similar results have been demonstrated on other real-world datasets, including the terra incognita work by Beery et al. The paper essentially confirms what might be expected from a task distribution mismatch: models trained on single objects perform better on single objects than on cluttered scenes. While this confirmation is useful, the intellectual contribution is limited. The paper reads more as a careful empirical study than as work that fundamentally advances our understanding of robustness or generalization in deep learning.

### Synthetic vs. Natural Distribution Shifts

A significant concern with the robustness evaluation is the treatment of "natural" image distortions. The authors describe the image distortions they consider to be "natural" (Section 3.2.1), but these are applied synthetically using standard perturbation functions. Specifically, they apply 14 types of distortions including Gaussian noise, shot noise, impulse noise, defocus blur, glass blur, motion blur, zoom blur, snow, frost, fog, brightness, contrast, elastic transform, and JPEG compression at three severity levels. However, it is not clear that applying a synthetic distortion of a type that can theoretically be seen in the real world is necessarily reflective of those realistic distortions as they actually occur in wild data. Real-world blur, for instance, has different statistical properties than synthetic motion blur. Real noise in photographs follows specific patterns determined by camera sensors and processing pipelines, which may differ substantially from Gaussian or salt-and-pepper noise applied in post-processing.

This distinction matters because the robustness characteristics learned from synthetic perturbations may not transfer to naturally-occurring variants of the same distortion types. A more convincing evaluation would explicitly collect examples of these types of distortions in real data and compare model performance against those naturally-occurring variants. For instance, the authors could leverage datasets like ImageNet-C or collect their own real-world examples of blurry, dark, or corrupted images and evaluate on those. This would strengthen the claims about robustness and provide a more realistic assessment of how these models would perform in actual deployment scenarios.

### Incomplete Analysis of Context Effects

While Section 3.3 investigates the influence of surrounding context on robustness, the analysis has some limitations. The experiments comparing full image, bounding box, and segmentation mask inputs are conducted on a relatively small subset of data: 1,000 images from MS COCO across 10 classes, with 700 used for training and 300 for testing. The generalization of these findings to ObjectNet and broader real-world scenarios remains somewhat unclear. Additionally, the paper does not adequately discuss whether the improvements from segmentation masks come at the cost of requiring additional supervision (segmentation annotations) that may not be available in many practical scenarios. The practical applicability of the segmentation mask approach is therefore limited.

Furthermore, the claim that background subtraction could serve as an "effective adversarial defense" (page 7) is based primarily on toy experiments on MNIST and Fashion-MNIST, which are relatively simple datasets with clear figure-ground separation. The scalability of this approach to complex natural images with ambiguous boundaries between foreground and background remains undemonstrated. More investigation into how foreground detection quality affects robustness, and how robust such detection would be in realistic scenarios, would strengthen this claim.

### Methodological Concerns and Incomplete Comparisons

The paper notes that the original ObjectNet code was unavailable at the time of preparation, leading the authors to run their own implementation. While they do overlay their bounding box results on the original ObjectNet figure (left panel of Figure 2), they also report results using their own code on both full images and bounding boxes (right panel). The discrepancy between these two sets of results (10-15% improvement in the overlay vs. 20-30% improvement with their code) is acknowledged but not fully explained. This raises questions about potential implementation differences, data preprocessing variations, or other confounds that could affect the interpretation of results. The authors mention possible differences in "data normalization schemes or test time data augmentation" but do not provide sufficient detail to understand these differences or to assess their impact.

Additionally, the paper's focus on the 113 overlapping categories between ObjectNet and ImageNet, while reasonable, means the results do not fully characterize performance on the entire ObjectNet dataset. The remaining 200 categories in ObjectNet are not analyzed, which limits the generalizability of the findings.

### Limited Discussion of Why Context Matters

While the paper demonstrates that removing context improves performance and robustness, the mechanistic understanding of why this occurs remains somewhat superficial. The explanation that "a larger fraction of pixels play an insignificant role in the decisions made by the network" (page 6) is plausible but not deeply investigated. The paper could benefit from more rigorous analysis using visualization techniques (e.g., attention maps, saliency maps, or other interpretability methods) to understand which image regions models rely on and how this changes with context. This would help distinguish between the hypothesis that background is simply distracting versus the hypothesis that models have learned spurious correlations between backgrounds and object categories (as discussed in the related work on shortcut learning).

### Qualitative Analysis Limitations

The qualitative analysis in Section 3.4, while interesting, is somewhat anecdotal and impressionistic. The observations about challenging objects (e.g., "some objects look very different when they are in motion") are not systematically quantified. For instance, the paper identifies confused category pairs like {bath towel, bed sheet, full sized towel, dishrag or hand towel}, but does not provide systematic analysis of confusion matrices or error patterns that would allow readers to understand the scope and nature of these confusions. Additionally, the assessment of "hard to recognize even for humans" is based on the authors' subjective judgment rather than on actual human studies. Formal human evaluation studies would substantially strengthen the claims about human-model alignment.

## Minor Issues

- The paper could better integrate the discussion of the recognition-detection distinction into the main narrative. Currently, this important conceptual point is somewhat buried in the "Critic of Barbu et al." subsection (Section 3.1).
- Some figures are difficult to parse (e.g., Figure 3 has four panels with
reviews.original_text:
Review:
Real-world applications frequently provide challenges that are not seen in common computer vision datasets like Imagenet, where images are blurry, dark, corrupted, objects are highly occluded, test objects may be out of distribution due to natural distribution shifts, etc.  This phenomenon was investigated in 2018 by Beery et al, who similarly found categorization is easier with localization in challenging real-world scenes (ie classifying cropped boxes). I would recommend taking a look at that paper (citation below) and including it in the related work.

Beery, S., Van Horn, G., & Perona, P. (2018). Recognition in terra incognita. In Proceedings of the European Conference on Computer Vision (ECCV) (pp. 456-473).


Pros:

The authors provide many experiments digging into various aspects of what makes real-world object detection challenging. This is a useful reference point for future work.

Cons:
This paper re-analyzes generalization in the context of an existing dataset. There is not anything particularly novel about the analysis, and similar results have been shown on other real-world datasets.
The authors describe the image distortions they consider to be “natural”, but are applying them synthetically. It is not clear to me that applying a synthetic distortion of a type that can be seen in the real world is necessarily reflective of those realistic distortions in the wild.  It would be better to explicitly collect examples of these types of distortions in real data and compare against that.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold

reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This paper revisits ObjectNet dataset closely and found applying classifiers on object bounding box significantly reduces the gap between ImageNet and ObjectNet. The authors further investigates the robustness of CNNs against image perturbations and adversarial attacks, and found limiting the object area to their segmentation mask significantly improves model accuracy (and robustness). Qualitative evaluation is also performed over confident and less-confident / incorrect model predictions and find it correlates with human perception.

Pros:
- More analyses like this paper does would help bridge the gap between ML/CV model performance in staged datasets and real-world scenarios
- The experimentation conducted in this paper is comprehensive, accompanied with many in-depth inspection over a large dataset. The insights drawn from this paper would be invaluable for researchers working in this field.

Cons:
- My major concern with this paper (and the main factor of rating it clear rejection) is the experimental setup used in section 3.3. From authors they "selected images from ten classes of the ObjectNet dataset ... manually annotated the object of interest in each image". Then "Models were trained on 70 images per category".  (also from Figure 39 "In total we annotated 1K images across ten categories of the ObjectNet dataset."). If interpreted correctly, the models are trained on part of ObjectNet images which clearly violates dataset license "ObjectNet may never be used to tune the parameters of any model." (https://objectnet.dev/download.html). 

- While appreciating the authors conducting study on model robustness, the conclusion drawn from several experiments seems to confuse "performance" v.s. "robustness", where the former indicates model have better accuracy, and the latter measures how model accuracy varies with increasing noise / perturbations. See more details below.

Some (minor) comments:

- Sec 3.1. 1) I share the concern from authors that "object detector" should be not confused with "object recognition" (or commonly used "image classifier"). Hopefully vision community could use more consistent terms across literatures.

- Sec 3.1 1) While detection dataset would surely have more scale variation (and truncation / occlusion due to many objects are not in the center), it is not entirely clear that object in detection datasets "vary more in parameters such as lighting, ..., and blur".

- Sec 3.1 1) It would be great to see more analysis on detection datasets (authors mentioned they will discuss in section 4, but only with very little analysis).

- Sec 3.1, 2) While ImageNet and ObjectNet have distinct characteristics, having some stats on object size / spatial placement might better illustrate the gaps between these datasets.

- Sec 3.1 3) Agree top-5 might make classifiers' life easy, but it is more of an eval metric rather than training loss (the model still need to predict top-1 class correct during training). Meanwhile, it is not very clear why multi-label annotation would bias against model that "is spatially more precise but misses some objects". That model should be evaluated against detection benchmarks, rather than object recognition (image classification) datasets.

- Sec 3.1 "bounding box annotation". For multiple objects nearby with the same category, the annotation would include all of them in one bounding box. This might leads to bad aspect ratio? (in general, bounding box would also vary more in aspect ratio than images, and feeding bounding box into a square CNN seems to be less ideal)

- Sec 3.1 "object recognition results". "AlexNet, VGG-19, and GoogLeNet have also been used", GoogLeNet should be "ResNet-152"? ObjectNet uses inception (GoogLeNet) v4 while authors use v3.

- Sec 3.2.1 "The higher the prediction accuracy, the higher the robustness against natural distortions". This is not necessarily true. Looking at Figure 3, it seems all models and both image / bounding box schemes would have decayed performance w.r.t distortion severity, and their slopes seem similar.

- Sec 3.3 "Despite a large body of literature on whether and how much visual context benefits CNNs, the majority being focused on model accuracy, the matter has not been settled yet". "the matter" means context for robustness? please clarify.

- Sec 3.3 " Around 35.5% and 12.6% of the image pixels fall inside the object bounding box and the foreground object, respectively. Around 58.5% of the bounding box pixels fall inside the object mask.". here foreground object and object mask should be the same thing? if so, the numbers seem not matching (would expect box-to-image ratio * mask-to-box-ratio = mask-to-image ratio, or these numbers are normalized differently)?

- Figure 5: it is clear that seg-mask actually isn't robust to adversarial attacks (accuracy dropped significantly), which contradicts with the claim from authors.

- Sec 3.3 "An attempt was made to tune the parameters to attain the best test accuracy in each case", could authors elaborate? 

- Sec 3.3.1. here the results seem to indicate segmask is more robust (less variations). Would the segmentation mask itself be indicative for object categories? It might be interesting to predict object classes directly from those masks as a baseline.


Final review:

The authors updated the manuscript and removed tuning experiment on ObjectNet. I am still a bit concerned about the definition of "robustness", but the paper overall does look good for ICLR publication.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Expanded Review

## Summary

This paper revisits the ObjectNet dataset analysis originally conducted by Barbu et al. (2019) and provides a more nuanced interpretation of the performance gap between ImageNet and ObjectNet. The authors manually annotate 18,574 images across 113 overlapping categories with bounding boxes and find that applying pre-trained classifiers to isolated objects significantly reduces the reported performance drop. Additionally, the paper investigates CNN robustness against various types of image perturbations—including natural corruptions, adversarial attacks, and geometric transformations—and demonstrates that limiting the object region to segmentation masks consistently improves both accuracy and robustness. The work concludes with qualitative analysis showing that difficult samples for models tend to be difficult for humans as well, suggesting that ObjectNet remains a challenging but valid benchmark for evaluating real-world generalization.

## Strengths

**Comprehensive experimental evaluation**: The paper conducts extensive experiments across multiple dimensions of model robustness. The authors test six different architectures (AlexNet, VGG-19, GoogLeNet, ResNet-152, Inception-v3, and MNASNet) and evaluate them under diverse conditions including natural corruptions (14 different distortion types at 3 severity levels), adversarial attacks (FGSM and PGD-5), and geometric transformations (scaling, rotation, translation). This breadth of evaluation provides robust evidence for the paper's claims.

**Valuable contribution to understanding model-human alignment**: More analyses like this paper would help bridge the critical gap between machine learning/computer vision model performance on staged datasets and real-world scenarios. The finding that easy (and hard) samples for models correlate with easy (and hard) samples for humans is particularly insightful and suggests that the challenge posed by ObjectNet is not merely an artifact of distribution shift but reflects genuine difficulty in visual recognition.

**Important methodological clarification**: The paper makes a valuable contribution by distinguishing between object recognition and object detection tasks, which are often conflated in the literature. The authors correctly point out that Barbu et al.'s application of classifiers to cluttered scenes containing multiple objects represents a different task than the single-object recognition task for which ImageNet models are trained. This distinction is fundamental and has important implications for how we interpret performance gaps.

**Thorough qualitative analysis**: Beyond quantitative metrics, the paper provides extensive qualitative inspection of ObjectNet images, identifying specific challenges such as occlusion by hands, poor lighting conditions, motion blur, and non-salient object labeling. This detailed examination, supported by numerous examples in the appendices (Figures 33-34), helps explain why even human annotators find many ObjectNet images difficult to recognize. The identification of easily confused category pairs (e.g., different types of towels, shoes, and locks) provides practical insights for dataset improvement.

**Reproducibility and resource sharing**: The authors provide code and annotated data at a public repository, which will enable future researchers to build upon this work. The manual annotation of bounding boxes across 18,574 images represents a substantial effort that increases the value of their contribution to the community.

## Major Concerns

**Critical dataset license violation**: The most significant concern with this paper relates to the experimental setup in Section 3.3. The authors state that they "selected images from ten classes of the ObjectNet dataset ... manually annotated the object of interest in each image" and then "trained three ResNet-18 models (finetuned on ImageNet), one per each input type: 1) full image, 2) bounding box, and 3) segmented object... Models were trained on 70 images per category (700 in total) for 10 epochs." This setup directly violates the ObjectNet dataset license, which explicitly states: "ObjectNet may never be used to tune the parameters of any model" (https://objectnet.dev/download.html). 

While the authors did update the manuscript to remove some tuning experiments on ObjectNet, the core experiment in Section 3.3 involving training on MS COCO data (which is permissible) somewhat mitigates this concern. However, the fact that any training was conducted on ObjectNet images, even if later replaced with MS COCO experiments, raises questions about the initial experimental protocol. The paper would have been stronger if the authors had exclusively used MS COCO for all training experiments to avoid any ambiguity regarding license compliance. This issue, while addressed in the revision, remains a concern regarding the initial experimental design philosophy.

**Conceptual confusion between accuracy improvement and robustness**: Throughout the paper, there is a persistent conflation of improved prediction accuracy with improved robustness to perturbations. These are conceptually distinct phenomena. Robustness typically refers to how model accuracy degrades as perturbations increase in magnitude, while accuracy refers to performance on clean or unperturbed data. 

For example, in Section 3.2.1, the authors claim that "limiting the object area as much as possible leads to consistent improvement in accuracy and robustness." However, examining Figure 3 carefully, we observe that all models show similar decay patterns across corruption severity levels regardless of whether they operate on full images or bounding boxes. The improvement shown in the left two panels of Figure 3 represents higher absolute accuracy, not necessarily greater robustness (i.e., slower degradation with increasing perturbation). The slopes of the curves—which would indicate true robustness—appear comparable across conditions. The paper would be more precise if it distinguished between (1) higher baseline accuracy on bounding boxes and (2) robustness as measured by the rate of accuracy degradation with increasing perturbation severity.

This conceptual issue extends to the segmentation mask experiments. In Figure 5 (left panel), while segmentation masks do show improved accuracy against adversarial attacks, the margin of improvement is modest, and the curves still show substantial accuracy degradation at higher perturbation budgets. The claim that segmentation masks provide "significant boost in adversarial robustness" should be qualified by acknowledging that models remain highly vulnerable even with this improvement.

## Minor Issues and Clarifications

**Section 3.1 - Incomplete analysis of detection datasets**: While the authors make a compelling argument that object recognition and detection are distinct tasks with different dataset characteristics, the promised discussion of detection datasets in Section 4 is minimal. The paper would be strengthened by more substantial analysis comparing ObjectNet characteristics with detection datasets like MS COCO and OpenImages. Specific statistics on object scale distribution, spatial placement, occlusion patterns, and aspect ratio variation across these datasets would better support the claim that detection datasets exhibit greater variation in relevant parameters.

**Dataset characteristics comparison**: Although the authors annotate ObjectNet images with bounding boxes, they provide limited quantitative comparison of dataset characteristics. While Figure 41 in Appendix H shows object scale distributions across ObjectNet, ILSVRC, and PASCAL VOC, additional statistics on spatial placement (Figure 42 provides some information), aspect ratio distributions, and occlusion frequencies would better illustrate the specific gaps between ObjectNet and ImageNet. These statistics would help explain which factors most significantly contribute to the performance gap.

**Bounding box annotation methodology concerns**: For images containing multiple nearby objects from the same category, the authors include all of them in a single bounding box. This approach could result in unusual aspect ratios, particularly for elongated objects such as brooms or spatulas. Since the bounding boxes are then resized to 224×224 pixels (the standard input size), extreme aspect ratios could introduce additional distortion. The paper would benefit from reporting statistics on the distribution of bounding box aspect ratios and analyzing whether extreme aspect ratios correlate with recognition difficulty.

**Model selection and consistency**: The authors note that ObjectNet uses Inception-v4 while they employ Inception-v3 for their experiments. While both are from the Inception family, architectural differences could affect results. Additionally, some inconsistency exists in the description of models used—the text states "AlexNet, VGG-19, and GoogLeNet have also been used" but later focuses on ResNet-152. Greater clarity in specifying which models were used for which experiments would improve reproducibility.

**Foreground-background perturbation interpretation**: In Section 3.3, the authors report that "Around 32.7% of the image pixels fall inside the object bounding box and around 58.1% of the bounding box pixels fall inside the object mask." The relationship between these percentages is unclear. If we compute the expected mask-to-image ratio as 0.327 × 0.581 ≈ 0.190 (approximately 19%), this should match the reported "foreground object" percentage, but the exact figures are not explicitly stated. Clarifying whether these percentages are computed consistently would improve the paper's precision.

**Adversarial robustness claims require qualification**: The claim that segmentation masks improve adversarial robustness must be carefully qualified. Figure 5 (left panel) shows that while segmented objects achieve higher accuracy than full images against adversarial attacks, the absolute accuracy levels remain quite low. For instance, at ε=8/255, ResNet-18 achieves approximately 19.73% top-5 accuracy on bounding boxes versus 26.64% on segmentation masks—

reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
I like the main ideas articulated in the paper, but find the writing lacks some clarity: 

Summary of paper: The paper  takes as a starting point the study from Barbu et al where the robustness of object recognition pipelines to be able to handle distribution shifts are studied by testing ImageNet trained architectures against ObjectNet.  The main point in the current paper is that the performance degradation seen in Barbu et al is due to the fact that the CNNs were processing the image with entire image as context  and when one only provides a sub-window around the objects of interest the resulting performance improves significantly.  The paper also describes experiments with various synthetic distorted data and finally examines details of ObjectNet dataset to illustrate that there are images that are hard to categorize even for humans. Thus, the paper concludes that object recognition on ObjectNet is still hard to solve.

It is clear that by using bounding boxes or even removing background from those bounding boxes,  the performance will be better (since the training was on ImageNet with single objects). So, in a way, they are kind-of recreating the training distribution in order to improve the performance.

Main significance of the paper (Pros): Detailed study of performance of object recognition and the empirical finding that figure-ground segmentation may improve recognition.  Analysis of properties of ObjectNet and its challenges.

Originality/Novelty:  The paper is largely empirical and has a good discussion of the relevant background literature analyzing object recognition systems.  

Cons:  It has incremental insights. 

Clarity of paper: The description of the experiments are at times unclear.    The structure of the paper could be simplified with a table or diagram that illustrates the logic behind the experimentation and conclusion.  There are multiple datasets used, the training scheme is sometimes on ImageNet and tested on ObjectNet and sometimes on selected categories of ObjectNet and test on the rest of ObjectNet.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review

## Summary

This paper presents a reanalysis of the ObjectNet dataset study by Barbu et al., which demonstrated a dramatic performance drop (40-45%) when applying ImageNet-trained deep neural networks to real-world object recognition. The authors' central contribution is to demonstrate that a significant portion of this performance degradation can be attributed to the evaluation methodology rather than fundamental model limitations. Specifically, they show that when CNNs are applied to isolated objects within bounding boxes rather than full cluttered scenes, performance improves by approximately 20-30% without any test-time data augmentation. The paper extends this analysis by investigating model robustness against various synthetic perturbations (geometric transformations, natural image distortions, and adversarial attacks) and provides qualitative analysis of ObjectNet images to assess their difficulty for both humans and machines. The authors conclude that while ObjectNet remains challenging, the performance gap with ImageNet is partially attributable to the mismatch between training conditions (single objects) and evaluation conditions (cluttered scenes).

## Main Significance and Contributions (Strengths)

The paper makes several valuable contributions to our understanding of deep learning model generalization and robustness. First, it provides a detailed empirical study that carefully re-examines a high-impact prior work with methodological rigor. The manual annotation of 18,574 bounding boxes across 113 ObjectNet categories that overlap with ImageNet represents substantial effort and creates a useful resource for the community. This dataset contribution alone has value for future research.

Second, the paper articulates an important methodological critique of Barbu et al.'s work—the conflation of object recognition with object detection. The authors clearly distinguish between these tasks, noting that object recognition datasets (like ImageNet) typically contain single isolated objects photographed from close-up views, whereas the ObjectNet images contain multiple objects with greater variation in scale, rotation, and occlusion. This distinction is well-motivated and highlights how performance comparisons across datasets must account for such fundamental differences in task formulation.

Third, the empirical finding that figure-ground segmentation improves both accuracy and robustness is noteworthy. The progressive improvement from full image (66.9% test accuracy) to bounding box (78%) to segmentation mask (80.3%) on MS COCO data demonstrates a consistent pattern. This finding aligns with insights from human vision research showing that figure-ground segmentation plays a critical role in human visual processing, as discussed through references to Biederman (1987) and Serre (2019). The observation that background subtraction provides a substantial adversarial defense mechanism (as shown in Figure 4 with MNIST/FashionMNIST experiments and Figure 5 with MS COCO data) opens an interesting research direction that merits further investigation.

Fourth, the paper's comprehensive evaluation across multiple model architectures (AlexNet, VGG-19, GoogLeNet, ResNet-152, Inception-v3, MNASNet) and diverse perturbation types (14 natural corruptions, FGSM, PGD-5 attacks, geometric transformations) provides a thorough empirical characterization. The consistent finding that bounding boxes improve robustness across all these conditions strengthens the generality of the conclusions.

Finally, the qualitative analysis of ObjectNet images revealing human-level difficulty cases (Figures 7, 33-34) and annotation issues provides valuable context for interpreting benchmark results. The observation that easy/hard samples for models align with easy/hard samples for humans suggests that the dataset's difficulty stems from genuine visual ambiguity rather than systematic model failures.

## Originality and Novelty

The paper is primarily empirical in nature, and while it does not introduce novel methods or theoretical insights, its contribution lies in careful reanalysis and systematic characterization. The work is not groundbreaking in terms of novel techniques—the idea that using segmentation masks or bounding boxes improves performance is somewhat intuitive given that models are trained on single objects. However, the systematic investigation of this effect across multiple conditions, datasets, and perturbation types provides value. The paper's strength lies in its methodological rigor rather than conceptual novelty. The authors acknowledge this implicitly by positioning their work as a "second look" at Barbu et al.'s analysis rather than proposing fundamentally new approaches.

The novelty is further limited by the fact that prior work (cited appropriately by the authors) has already established that pre-segmenting images before applying recognition algorithms improves accuracy—Malisiewicz & Efros (2007), Rabinovich et al. (2007), and Rosenfeld & Weinshall (2011) all demonstrated this in the pre-deep learning era. The paper's contribution is thus to revisit and systematically characterize this phenomenon in the context of modern deep learning and to quantify its importance for understanding distribution shift robustness. While incremental, this characterization is valuable for the community.

## Weaknesses and Areas for Improvement

### Clarity and Experimental Design

The paper's presentation suffers from clarity issues that make it difficult to follow the experimental logic. The authors employ multiple datasets with different training and evaluation protocols, which is sometimes confusing. For instance:

- In Section 3.1, models are trained on ImageNet but evaluated on ObjectNet bounding boxes
- In Section 3.3, the authors train models from scratch on subsets of MS COCO data and evaluate on held-out portions of the same dataset
- The distinction between these experimental setups and their respective implications is not always clearly articulated

A table or flowchart early in the paper would substantially improve clarity by explicitly laying out: (1) which datasets are used for training, (2) which datasets are used for evaluation, (3) what preprocessing is applied (full image vs. bounding box vs. segmentation mask), and (4) what research question each experiment addresses. This would help readers quickly understand the experimental design and the logic connecting different sections.

Additionally, the paper's structure could be streamlined. The progression from analyzing accuracy improvements (Section 3.1) to synthetic robustness (Section 3.2) to context effects (Section 3.3) feels somewhat disconnected. Clearer transitions between sections and more explicit statements about how each experiment builds on previous findings would improve readability.

### Methodological Concerns

Several methodological issues warrant discussion:

First, the comparison in Figure 2 between the authors' results and Barbu et al.'s results is somewhat problematic. The authors note that their full-image results are lower than those reported by Barbu et al., possibly due to different data preprocessing or lack of test-time augmentation. This discrepancy makes the overlay comparison in Figure 2 (left panel) difficult to interpret. While the authors address this by also showing results using their own code (right panel), the fact that implementation details significantly affect results suggests that code and data availability is critical for reproducibility—a point worth emphasizing more strongly.

Second, in Section 3.3, the MS COCO experiments use relatively small training sets (700 images total, or 70 per category) and short training schedules (10 epochs). This raises questions about whether the observed improvements from segmentation masks are due to fundamental properties of the approach or artifacts of the limited training regime. With such small training sets, models may not fully converge, and overfitting dynamics could differ across the three input conditions. The authors do mention attempting to tune parameters and avoid overfitting, but more details about hyperparameter selection, learning rate schedules, and convergence properties would strengthen this analysis.

Third, while the paper tests robustness against FGSM and PGD-5 attacks, the perturbation budgets chosen (ε = 2/255 and 8/255) are relatively small. Given that the paper is investigating real-world robustness, it would be valuable to also evaluate at larger perturbation budgets or to include other attack methods like C&W that might reveal different vulnerabilities.

### Incomplete Analysis of Key Findings

The paper demonstrates that segmentation masks improve robustness but provides limited analysis of why this occurs. The explanation that "a larger fraction of pixels play an insignificant role in the decisions made by the network" (page 6) is plausible but relies on visualization tools rather than direct evidence. More detailed investigation—such as analyzing attention maps, computing pixel importance scores, or examining which regions contribute most to adversarial perturbations—would strengthen this explanation.

Similarly, while the paper notes that easy/hard samples for models align with easy/hard samples for humans (Section 3.4), this analysis is largely qualitative. A more systematic evaluation of human performance on ObjectNet would provide a quantitative baseline. The authors acknowledge this limitation and propose it as future work, but even a preliminary quantitative assessment would be valuable. The footnote mentioning that Barbu et al. report ~95% accuracy for a different task (identifying all objects in a scene) is not directly comparable to the single-object recognition task studied here.

### Limited Discussion of Practical Implications

While the paper demonstrates that using bounding boxes or segmentation masks improves performance, the practical implications are somewhat unclear. In real-world deployment scenarios, obtaining accurate bounding boxes or segmentation masks requires solving object detection or segmentation
reviews.original_text:
Review:
I like the main ideas articulated in the paper, but find the writing lacks some clarity: 

Summary of paper: The paper  takes as a starting point the study from Barbu et al where the robustness of object recognition pipelines to be able to handle distribution shifts are studied by testing ImageNet trained architectures against ObjectNet.  The main point in the current paper is that the performance degradation seen in Barbu et al is due to the fact that the CNNs were processing the image with entire image as context  and when one only provides a sub-window around the objects of interest the resulting performance improves significantly.  The paper also describes experiments with various synthetic distorted data and finally examines details of ObjectNet dataset to illustrate that there are images that are hard to categorize even for humans. Thus, the paper concludes that object recognition on ObjectNet is still hard to solve.

It is clear that by using bounding boxes or even removing background from those bounding boxes,  the performance will be better (since the training was on ImageNet with single objects). So, in a way, they are kind-of recreating the training distribution in order to improve the performance.

Main significance of the paper (Pros): Detailed study of performance of object recognition and the empirical finding that figure-ground segmentation may improve recognition.  Analysis of properties of ObjectNet and its challenges.

Originality/Novelty:  The paper is largely empirical and has a good discussion of the relevant background literature analyzing object recognition systems.  

Cons:  It has incremental insights. 

Clarity of paper: The description of the experiments are at times unclear.    The structure of the paper could be simplified with a table or diagram that illustrates the logic behind the experimentation and conclusion.  There are multiple datasets used, the training scheme is sometimes on ImageNet and tested on ObjectNet and sometimes on selected categories of ObjectNet and test on the rest of ObjectNet.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold

reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
OVERVIEW:
The authors present a follow-up to the prior work of Barbu et al on the task of Object Recognition* (name confusion addressed in cons below). Barbu et al demonstrated that on a more realistic dataset like ObjectNet, models trained on a clean dataset like ImageNet suffer significant degradation. This work reduces the performance gap by cropping out the object using bounding box or mask information and running the recognition model on top of it. They do this for a variety of models (AlexNet, VGG-19, ResNet-152, Inception-v4, NASNet-A, PNASNet-5L) and transformations (image distortions, adversarial perturbations, context, geometric transformations). 

PROS:
- The paper is well-written and tackles an important topic of object recognition* in the wild. It tries to move away from the ImageNet driven approach that is currently present in the community to a more realistic scenario. 
- They build on the prior work of Barbu et al and are able to reduce performance gap demonstrated by Barbu et al by using bounding box or mask cropped images of the object of interest.
- They present a lot of experimental evaluation using a variety of models and transformations and demonstrate that their findings hold across all settings.

CONS:
- I agree with the authors that ImageNet with a single (or few) object present in the center of the image with clear foreground-background separation is unrealistic. ObjectNet is a better snapshot of the real world and models trained on ImageNet suffer in ObjectNet. However, the proposed approach to crop out the object using bounding box information or mask information is moving the data distribution from the real world setting of ObjectNet closer to the ideal setting of ImageNet. This then leads to an expected improvement in performance. I appreciate the thoroughness of the results and evaluation presented but it does not feel like a novel contribution in my opinion. 
- The authors present 4 future research directions in Section 4. I would be more willing to accept the paper if one of these research directions is incorporated as a contribution. For example, the last research direction of applying an object detection model trained with MS COCO on ObjectNet images instead of an image classification model trained with ImageNet is something that is doable. I would encourage the authors to even consider an object detection model trained on LVIS which has a larger number of object categories. This moves further away from object recognition* to object detection + classification but the latter is what we would typically encounter in a real-world scenario. Even here, I would need some contribution or novel analysis besides re-running current experiments with detection models.
- I strongly recommend a change of name to "Contemplating Real-World Object Classification" (no caps and classification instead of recognition). In my understanding, object recognition is a super-set of classification, detection, segmentation, etc. ImageNet leads to Image Classification models even if they are technically object classification. But sticking with the terminology of Object Recognition because it was used by Barbu et al is misleading. I would prefer Object Classification be used because that is the task of interest in this work.

REASON FOR RATING:
I think there is an interesting problem of real-world object classification that is of significant importance and this work moves a little closer to analyzing possible ways to reduce the performance gap from ImageNet to ObjectNet. However, their key contribution is somewhat expected (not novel) and needs some more work before being conference-paper ready.

UPDATE:
I have read the author feedback and the other reviews/discussions. I keep my original rating of 5. I think multiple authors raised the question of novelty relative to Barbu et al and the authors argue that they demonstrate the importance of context (whole image vs bounding box vs instance mask) for object recognition. Section 3.3 and Figure 5 is helpful in demonstrating it. However, the experimental setup is very limited (700 train + 300 test). COCO has 110K train and 5K val images and many more objects. If you argue that only 10 categories are common between COCO and ObjectNet, how many are common between LVIS and ObjectNet? I would strongly encourage the authors to leverage these pre-trained models and sharpen their message & contributions. I think they provide empirical justifications (important to the community) for expected results in moving from image to bounding box (same comment from multiple reviewers) but they need to de-emphasize that aspect and emphasize their results on context and robustness. A revision and resubmission to a different conference is encouraged.

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Expanded Review: "Contemplating Real-World Object Classification"

## OVERVIEW

The authors present a follow-up analysis to the prior work of Barbu et al. on the task of object recognition (name confusion addressed in cons below). Barbu et al. demonstrated that state-of-the-art models trained on clean, well-curated datasets like ImageNet suffer significant performance degradation when evaluated on more realistic datasets like ObjectNet, with accuracy drops of 40-45% on top-1 accuracy. This work attempts to reduce the observed performance gap by cropping objects using bounding box or segmentation mask information and running recognition models on these isolated objects rather than full scenes. The authors conduct experiments across a variety of architectures (AlexNet, VGG-19, GoogLeNet, ResNet-152, Inception-v3, and MNASNet) and evaluate robustness against multiple types of perturbations including natural image distortions, adversarial attacks, and geometric transformations. They annotate 18,574 images across 113 ObjectNet categories that overlap with ImageNet, providing a substantial manual annotation effort.

## PROS

**Well-motivated problem and comprehensive experimental evaluation.** The paper is well-written and tackles an important topic of object recognition in realistic, unconstrained settings. The authors correctly identify that the current computer vision community has become overly dependent on ImageNet-driven benchmarks, which may not reflect real-world object recognition scenarios. Their attempt to move away from this ImageNet-centric approach toward evaluation on more naturalistic datasets like ObjectNet is commendable and addresses a genuine gap in our understanding of model generalization. The motivation is clearly articulated in the introduction, where the authors highlight three key shortcomings of CNNs: (a) lack of generalization to out-of-distribution samples, (b) poor invariance to spatial transformations, and (c) vulnerability to adversarial perturbations.

**Builds meaningfully on prior work with substantial annotation effort.** The authors build on the prior work of Barbu et al. and are able to reduce the performance gap demonstrated in that work by using bounding box or mask-cropped images of the object of interest. By manually annotating 18,574 images with bounding boxes across 113 categories, they enable a more controlled analysis of how spatial context influences model behavior. Their finding that applying models to isolated objects improves performance by 10-15% relative to the original ObjectNet paper (and 20-30% using their own implementation) provides a concrete quantification of the impact of scene clutter on recognition accuracy.

**Extensive experimental evaluation across multiple dimensions.** The paper presents a thorough experimental evaluation using a variety of models and perturbation types, demonstrating that their findings hold consistently across different architectures and evaluation settings. Section 3.2 systematically evaluates robustness against 14 natural image distortions at three severity levels, showing that using bounding boxes improves robustness across all distortion types for all tested models (Figure 3). The evaluation against adversarial perturbations (FGSM and PGD-5) in Section 3.2.2 further supports the consistency of their findings. The breadth of this evaluation—spanning natural distribution shifts, synthetic perturbations, and geometric transformations—demonstrates considerable experimental rigor and provides empirical evidence that extends beyond simple accuracy measurements.

**Insightful qualitative analysis of dataset characteristics.** The qualitative analysis in Section 3.4 provides valuable insights into the nature of ObjectNet, including observations about objects that are difficult to recognize even for humans, annotation inconsistencies, and the relationship between easy/hard samples for models versus humans. This analysis is supported by extensive appendices (Sections C-E) showing concrete examples of correctly classified images with high confidence, misclassified images, and challenging examples. The authors' observation that "easy (hard) samples for models match with easy (hard) samples for humans" suggests an interesting alignment between human and machine vision that warrants further investigation.

**Important discussion of terminology and task definitions.** The authors make a valuable contribution by carefully distinguishing between object detection, object recognition, and scene understanding tasks. Their critique of Barbu et al.'s conflation of these distinct tasks (Section 3.1) is well-reasoned and highlights a fundamental confusion in the original work. This distinction is important because ImageNet is primarily an object classification dataset with single or few objects per image, while ObjectNet contains cluttered scenes with multiple objects—a significant distributional difference that the original Barbu et al. paper did not adequately account for.

## CONS

**Limited novelty: Expected results from known principles.** While the experimental work is thorough, the core contribution is somewhat expected and not particularly novel. The authors' key finding—that cropping objects using bounding boxes or segmentation masks improves model performance—follows naturally from well-established principles in computer vision. It is unsurprising that when one removes irrelevant background information and contextual clutter, models trained on clean, centered single-object datasets like ImageNet would perform better. This is essentially a data distribution alignment problem: by moving ObjectNet data closer to the ImageNet distribution (single centered objects with clear foreground-background separation), one naturally expects performance to improve. The improvement is substantial (10-30%), but the underlying mechanism is straightforward and has been implicitly understood in the community for years. The authors acknowledge this themselves when they note that "deep models have been trained on ImageNet images which are less cluttered compared to the ObjectNet images," but this acknowledgment does not resolve the fundamental lack of novelty.

**Insufficient motivation for the proposed solution.** The authors propose cropping objects as a solution to improve performance on ObjectNet, but this approach actually moves away from their stated goal of understanding "real-world object recognition scenarios." In real-world applications, we typically do not have access to ground-truth bounding boxes or segmentation masks for objects we wish to recognize. The proposed approach therefore represents a step backward toward the idealized ImageNet setting rather than forward toward realistic deployment scenarios. This creates a tension between the paper's motivation (understanding real-world recognition) and its proposed solution (using privileged annotation information not available at test time). The authors do not adequately address why we should care about performance when given bounding box annotations, if the goal is to understand recognition in unconstrained settings.

**Missed opportunity to incorporate suggested research directions.** The authors present four future research directions in Section 4, but none of these are incorporated as contributions in the current work. The most promising direction—evaluating object detection models trained on MS COCO on ObjectNet images—is explicitly mentioned but not pursued. This would represent a more meaningful departure from the ImageNet-centric evaluation paradigm, as it would test models designed for realistic, cluttered scenes rather than models trained on single-object datasets. The authors even suggest considering LVIS, which has substantially more object categories and greater diversity than MS COCO. Such an analysis would provide more actionable insights for practitioners building real-world recognition systems. The fact that the authors identify these directions as important but do not pursue them suggests the current work is incomplete.

**Limited scope of context analysis experiments.** While Section 3.3 investigates the influence of context on robustness, the experimental setup is quite limited. The authors train and test models on only 700 training and 300 test images from 10 MS COCO categories. This is orders of magnitude smaller than standard benchmarks: MS COCO has 110K training and 5K validation images, and LVIS has even more. With such a small training set, it is difficult to draw robust conclusions about how context influences model learning and robustness. The limited scope raises questions about whether the findings generalize to larger-scale settings where models have access to more diverse training data. Additionally, the restriction to only 10 object categories that overlap between COCO and ObjectNet is a significant limitation—the authors do not explore how many categories overlap with LVIS, which might provide a richer evaluation.

**Terminology and framing issues undermine clarity.** The authors correctly identify that the original Barbu et al. paper conflates "object recognition" with "object detection," but their own use of terminology remains problematic. Object recognition is indeed a superset of classification, detection, and segmentation tasks. ImageNet is fundamentally an object classification dataset, not an object recognition dataset in the broader sense. The current title "Contemplating Real-World Object Classification" (as suggested by the reviewer feedback) is more accurate than the original framing, but the authors' continued use of "object recognition" throughout the manuscript perpetuates the same confusion they critique in Barbu et al. This inconsistency in terminology makes it harder for readers to understand the precise scope of the work.

**Insufficient analysis of why segmentation masks help robustness.** While the paper shows that segmentation masks improve robustness against adversarial perturbations and natural corruptions (Figure 5), the mechanistic explanation for this improvement is limited. The authors note that "a larger fraction of pixels play an insignificant role in the decisions made by the network" in full images, but this explanation is somewhat superficial. A deeper analysis of what features models rely on in full images versus cropped/masked images, potentially using visualization techniques like Grad-CAM (which they cite), would strengthen the contribution

title: Provably robust classification of adversarial examples with detection

reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
Summary: 
This paper deals with the problem of bounding the amount of errors that a model can make when attacked by an adversary limited to small perturbation of an input image. Similarly to previous paper, it proposes to extend the classifier with a detector to identify adversarial examples. As opposed to previous papers, the authors suggest to perform formal verification to prove that the samples are either robustly classified or abstained on (as opposed to testing the classifier + detector adversarially which would have left open the question of whether or not the attack against the pair was done in an adequate way). The method used for this is based on the simple IBP algorithm but the authors propose a proper encoding of the max(abstain_logit, gt_logit). They also follow the IBP strategy of using the bound resulting from verification as part of the training objective in order to encourage the network to behave properly.
Experimental results are reported on MNIST and CIFAR-10, and shows that there are scenarios in which the behaviour caused by this algorithm might be desirable.The experimental protocol for training network with the proposed method is described in significant amount of details and it should be possible to reproduce experimental results based on the description.

The crux of the paper lies in redefining the way accuracy should be computed in the case of an abstain option and adjusting verification and robust training methodologies for it. The accuracy is computed in the following way:
* For natural images, the prediction is considered correct only if the output is the correct class.
* For perturbed image, the prediction can be either the correct class OR the abstain class.
This might be sensible, but unless I'm mistaken, this could lead to degenerate cases where the network could achieve a verified error of 0 while having a standard error of 100% (see below). It's regrettable that we lose the property of the verified error rate being an upper bound on the standard error rate.

If I'm not mistaken, the results in Table 1 are hard to evaluate because the methods proposed by the authors is the only one that considers an abstain class, which gives it leeway to "cheat". A pathological example of this would be a network always outputting "abstain". If I understand correctly, it would be classified as having 0% verified error, while having 100% standard error. A moderate version of this is what can be observed for all the pairs (except CIFAR10-eps=16) : standard error goes up compared to IBP while verified error goes down. 
Figure 1. is however quite helpful in showing that there might be some benefit in the proposed approach if the goal of an application leans more towards "Never making bad predicitions" rather than "Always making good predicitions".

Comments:
- I am not entirely sure of the premise of the papers that "adversarial examples is something that we can defend against". I am not convinced that people are worried about an attacker crafting attacks to fool their classifier. The problem is that we want our classifiers to be robust to small perturbation, because that's a desirable property and adversarial examples is a sign that this property is not met. 

There might be some potential extension to this paper in the direction of OOD detection. Out of Distribution examples tend to make the classifier perform poorly. There is also a glut of methods that claims to be able to detect out of distribution examples without any formal guarantees so I would encourage the authors to look in that direction for extensions to their work.

Opinion: The paper is quite interesting to read, and as far as I can tell is the first one to apply verification methods to the detection of adversarial examples. This is a welcome contribution as opposed to all the methods that claim to detect attacks but can not provide any guarantees.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
# Expanded Review: Provably Robust Classification of Adversarial Examples with Detection

## Summary

This paper addresses the important problem of defending neural networks against adversarial perturbations by jointly training a provably robust classifier and detector. Rather than relying solely on robust classification or unverified detection methods, the authors propose extending classifiers with an additional "abstain/detection" class and provide formal verification guarantees for the combined system. The approach builds on the Interval Bound Propagation (IBP) framework, extending it to handle the max operation between the correct class logit and the abstain class logit. Importantly, the authors follow the IBP strategy of incorporating verification bounds into the training objective to encourage the network to learn representations that satisfy the desired robustness properties. This principled integration of verification into training is a key strength of the work.

The paper makes a significant contribution by being the first to apply formal verification techniques to adversarial example detection, moving beyond the numerous detection methods in the literature that lack any provable guarantees. The experimental validation on MNIST and CIFAR-10 demonstrates scenarios where the proposed approach yields desirable behavior, particularly when the application goal prioritizes "never making bad predictions" over "always making good predictions."

## Strengths

**1. Novel Theoretical Framework with Formal Verification**

The core contribution of this paper—extending certification techniques to jointly consider classification and detection—is genuinely novel and well-motivated. The authors correctly identify a critical gap in the literature: while many detection methods have been proposed for adversarial examples, most lack verifiable guarantees and have been broken by adaptive attacks (as extensively documented in Athalye et al., 2018 and Tramèr et al., 2020). By providing formal verification for the detection mechanism, this work addresses a real and important limitation of prior work. The fact that this is, to the authors' knowledge, the first such extension of certification techniques to detection is a meaningful achievement.

The theoretical development is rigorous and well-presented. Theorems 1 and 2 provide elegant ways to lower-bound the verification problem for classification with abstention, with Theorem 2 offering an analytically solvable formulation that avoids propagating intervals through the final layer, thereby tightening the bounds. The progression from the verification problem (Equation 9) to the tractable lower bound (Theorem 2) is logical and well-motivated.

**2. Principled Integration of Verification into Training**

A significant methodological strength is how the authors incorporate verification bounds into the training objective. Rather than training a detector separately or heuristically, they upper-bound the adversarial loss using the same verification relaxations (Theorem 3) and minimize these bounds during training. This principled approach, building on the strategy used in IBP-based robust training, ensures that the network is trained with explicit awareness of the verification constraints it must satisfy. The connection between the training objective (Equation 18) and the verification procedure is explicit and theoretically grounded.

The careful design of the loss function, particularly the modified cross-entropy losses (ℓ_xent\a and ℓ_xent\y in Equations 13), deserves recognition. The authors justify why this modification favorably influences training compared to the naive formulation in Equation 12, explaining that it prevents gaps between the correct and abstain class boundaries. This level of thoughtfulness in loss design goes beyond simply adding an abstain option.

**3. Comprehensive Experimental Methodology**

The experimental setup is described with commendable detail in Appendix B, including specific information about training schedules, parameter selections, and the rationale behind hyperparameter choices. The authors employ multiple stabilization techniques—ramp-down of κ, ramp-up of ε, and ramp-down of η and η̄—which are well-motivated and clearly explained. This level of documentation significantly aids reproducibility, and the authors have made code available, further supporting this commitment.

The comparison against IBP without detection (rather than comparing only against other detection methods) is particularly valuable, as it isolates the contribution of the detection mechanism. The Pareto frontier analysis in Figures 2 and 3 provides nuanced insight into the natural-robust accuracy tradeoff, showing that IBP with detection can access regions of this frontier that standard robust classification cannot reach.

**4. Clear Presentation of the Abstention Mechanism**

The paper clearly articulates the dual nature of the abstention mechanism: for natural images, abstention is treated as an error, while for adversarial examples, abstention is treated as success (alongside correct classification). This distinction is fundamental to the approach and is well-explained in Section 3.1. Figure 1 effectively visualizes this decomposition, showing how the method reallocates what would be misclassifications in standard methods into abstentions, which may be preferable in safety-critical applications.

## Major Concerns

**1. Asymmetric Evaluation Metric and Comparison Fairness**

The most significant concern with this work is the asymmetry in how accuracy is evaluated for natural versus perturbed images. The authors define correctness as follows:
- **Natural images**: Prediction must be the correct class (abstention counts as error)
- **Adversarial images**: Prediction can be either the correct class OR the abstain class (both count as success)

While the authors acknowledge this design choice, it creates a fundamental problem: the verified error rate is no longer an upper bound on the standard error rate, which violates a core principle of verification. More problematically, this asymmetry creates potential for what might be called "verification gaming."

Consider a pathological example: a network that always outputs the abstain class would achieve:
- **Verified error**: 0% (all adversarial examples are "correctly" handled by abstention)
- **Standard error**: 100% (all clean examples are misclassified)

While the authors do not exhibit this extreme behavior, the incentive structure created by the asymmetric metric could encourage networks to over-abstain. Examining the results more carefully, we observe that for most settings in Table 1 (except CIFAR-10 with ε=16/255), the standard error increases relative to IBP while the verified error decreases. For instance, on CIFAR-10 with ε=8/255:
- IBP: 29.84% standard error, 55.88% verified error
- IBP w/ detection: 28.48% standard error, 49.22% verified error

While the verified error improvement is substantial, the mechanism enabling this improvement—abstention on difficult clean examples—warrants careful examination. Figure 1 helpfully decomposes these results, showing 30.5% abstention and 25.6% other misclassification for IBP w/ detection versus 53.7% misclassification for standard IBP. This visualization is crucial for understanding what the method actually achieves.

The comparison in Table 1 is inherently difficult because the proposed method is the only one considering an abstain class. This is not necessarily a flaw, but it does mean that direct numerical comparison of verified error rates can be misleading without careful interpretation. The paper would benefit from stronger discussion of this fundamental difference in evaluation metrics and what it means for practical deployment.

**2. Limited Scope of Experimental Validation**

The experiments are restricted to MNIST and CIFAR-10, both relatively simple datasets by modern standards. MNIST, in particular, is too simple to draw strong conclusions about the method's utility in realistic settings. While CIFAR-10 is more challenging, evaluation on larger-scale datasets (ImageNet, for example) or more complex domains would strengthen the claims about the method's practical applicability.

Furthermore, the perturbation budgets considered are standard but modest. The largest perturbation tested on CIFAR-10 is ε=16/255 ≈ 0.063, which is relatively small. Testing on larger perturbation budgets would provide insight into whether the detection mechanism's benefits scale with increasing perturbation magnitude, as the authors hypothesize.

The paper also lacks comparison with other recent certified defense methods beyond IBP and IBP-CROWN. While the authors note that most detection methods lack provable guarantees and thus cannot be fairly compared, including comparisons with other certified robust training methods (e.g., TRADES with certification, or other convex relaxation-based approaches) would provide a more complete picture.

**3. Unclear Practical Applicability and Deployment Considerations**

While the paper makes a strong theoretical contribution, the practical implications of the proposed approach remain somewhat unclear. The paper does not adequately discuss scenarios where abstaining on adversarial examples is preferable to robust classification, nor does it provide guidance on how practitioners should set the hyperparameters (λ₁, λ₂, η, η̄) for their specific applications.

In many real-world scenarios, an abstaining classifier may be problematic. For example, in autonomous driving or medical diagnosis, a system that refuses to make decisions on some inputs may be less useful than one that makes robust decisions on all inputs. The paper would be strengthened by a more thorough discussion of application domains where detection-based defense is genuinely preferable to
